course_description:
Enrolment Note:This course has maximum quota of 25 students per semester, seminars with less than 10 students may not run. Enrolment for Seminars opens in O Week following the Honours Orientation information session. Enrolment will close once the quota has been reached. This course provides students with the opportunity to gain in-depth knowledge of the primary research literature within the context of the wider theoretical framework in relation to relevant topics that vary from year to year.

This seminar will explore practical real-world problems that are linked to sensory perception

(i.e. vision, hearing, touch, taste, smell). Each topic area focuses on a di erent subset of

sensory processes but are linked together through the application psychophysical techniques

employed to explore and potentially solve these problems. For each problem, we will explore

the relevant literature and endeavour to find a solution. At the very least, we will propose

experiments that can shed light on the basic sensory processes involved. Some potential

problems include: Cybersickness and discomfort experienced in virtual reality systems;

Specifying the optimal visual contrast for safety features for low vision individuals; What are the

optimal conditions for 3D displays in applied settings like laparoscopic surgery? How can we

exploit multisensory integration (the influence of one sensory modality on another modality) to

influence the intensity of flavours?

restrictions: BPsySc(Hons), BSc(Hons) in Psychology, BA(Hons) in Psychology students only.

The timetable for this course is available on the

course_aims: This course aims to develop skills in critical evaluation and to deepen understanding of contemporary research on a topic in psychology.

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate a critical and in-depth understanding of key topics in an area of psychology.
Demonstrate an ability to communicate orally and in writing about a topic in an area of psychology.
Demonstrate an understanding of how different types of research methods can contribute to our understanding of an area of psychology.
Effectively engage with others to discuss and evaluate empirical research.

assessment_details.title: Active Seminar Participation
assessment_details.task_description: Active contribution to class discussions. Active participation in the seminar sessions is essential for the course. Just as presenters will be expected to lead group discussion, audience members will b expected to engage with the speaker and discuss the material being presented in a thoughtful and constructive manner. Your participation is each session will be assessed on a pass/fail basis based on the quality (not quantity) of your contribution. Assessment Criteria: Demonstrates deep engagement with course materials. Asks insightful questions and contributes to discussions. Engages respectfully and thoughtfully with peers. If you are unable to participate actively during the session for whatever reason, please discuss with me ahead of time. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
If students miss a seminar presentation, alternative arrangements will only be considered by the Course Coordinator in significant medical or compassionate circumstances. If a student misses a seminar they must contact the Course Coordinator before the seminar with appropriate documentation covering the date of the seminar as per the School of Psychology Student Guidelines.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessment_details.title: Reading Reflections
assessment_details.task_description: To encourage active in-session participation, you will be required to submit a brief reflection on each of the readings being covered in that week's session, prior to the session. The reflection should detail questions and/or comments that arose during your reading of the material (and can make for useful contributions to the in-session discussion). There is no word limit for the reflections, but they are expected to be brief (approximately 150-200 words per paper). Reading reflections are an excellent avenue for seeking clarification about any of the materials covered in the seminar. Reflections are individually graded as pass/fail. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Reading reflections are to be submitted via email to the course coordinator prior to the seminar each week.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessment_details.title: Oral Presentation
assessment_details.task_description: Each student will deliver a 20 minute oral presentation on a selected topic from the course, followed by a 10 minute Q&A session. Students may choose from the list of pre-approved publications or propose their own (subject to approval). Assessment Criteria: Clear and engaging presentation of research paper with little or no reliance on written notes. Ability to explain neuroscience concepts in an accessible way. Strong understanding of the quality of scientific evidence. Can identify the constraints that limit scientific inferences. More detail on giving a strong presentation will be given in class. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
If you are unable to complete your presentation you should submit your appropriate documentation (as per the extension policy) to your tutor and/or course coordinator to arrange for an alternate time to complete your presentation.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessment_details.title: Written Assessment: 2000 Word Essay
assessment_details.task_description: You are required to submit a 2000 (+/- 200 words) essay on one of the real world problem themes (not the one you presented a seminar on). Your essay must be based on three papers provided in the specific theme. You should review each of the three papers in detail. There should be no more than five or six papers in your reference list. How has the research informed us about the real world problem they address? What does the balance of evidence in your three papers say about a possible solution or theory? More detail on writing a strong essay will be provided in class. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submitted via Turnitin. Please retain a copy of your submission.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
For full details please review the School of Psychology Student Guidelines - Extensions and Deferred quiz/exam (the web link can be found on the Policies and Procedures tab)
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension .
assessment_details.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

learning_activities.topic: Introduction to Course - 1 Introduction to the course and an overview of themes 1 and 2: Multisensory integration and food; 3D imagery in applied settings.

Introduction to Course - 2 Introduction to the course part two: overview of themes 3, 4, and 5: -Visibility of safety appliances for low vision travellers -Cybersickness in virtual reality -Multisensory integration in virtual reality
Student Preparation (No Class) No class. Students prepare their presentations for the remaining weeks.
Presentation and Discussion - 1 Students will present and critically evaluate research papers. Class members will discuss/debate and aspect of the paper or related ideas.
